edges.description: Anterior segment maldevelopment elevates glaucoma risk.
intermediate_mechanisms: Maldevelopment of the anterior segment impairs aqueous humor drainage.

edges.description: Anterior segment dysgenesis manifests with posterior embryotoxon.
Anterior segment dysgenesis includes hypoplasia of the iris.
Anterior segment dysgenesis includes displacement of the pupil.
Systemic ARS involvement includes diagnostic dental abnormalities.
Systemic ARS involvement includes craniofacial abnormalities.
FOXC1-related systemic ARS can include hearing loss.
FOXC1-related systemic ARS can include structural cardiac defects.

evidence.reference_title: Sensorineural hearing loss and hypoplastic cochlea in Axenfeld-Rieger syndrome with FOXC1 mutation.
evidence.snippet: Axenfeld-Rieger syndrome (ARS) type 3 is a rare autosomal dominant disease, characterized by anterior segment dysgenesis of the eye, hearing loss, and cardiac defects.

evidence.reference_title: Cardiac anomalies in Axenfeld-Rieger syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Axenfeld-Rieger syndrome is a rare multi-system disorder associated with cardiac anomalies.

evidence.reference_title: Axenfeld-Rieger Syndrome.
evidence.snippet: This anomaly encompasses posterior embryotoxon, iris hypoplasia, polycoria, and corectopia.

evidence.reference_title: Axenfeld-Rieger syndrome: report on dental and craniofacial findings.
evidence.snippet: Axenfeld-Rieger syndrome is a rare autosomal dominant disorder characterized by various ocular and extraocular malformations. ... Significant cranio-dento-facial findings that have been observed are, teeth with short and dilacerated roots, hyperplastic frenums and underdeveloped maxilla.

evidence.reference_title: Gene-specific facial dysmorphism in Axenfeld-Rieger syndrome caused by FOXC1 and PITX2 variants.
evidence.snippet: Axenfeld-Rieger syndrome is a genetic condition characterized by ocular and systemic features. ... A thin upper lip (55.9%) and a prominent forehead (41.2%) were common facial features shared between both genes. Hypertelorism/telecanthus (81.8% vs 25.0%, p = 0.002) and low-set ears (31.8% vs 0.0%, p = 0.036) were significantly more prevalent in individuals with FOXC1 variants compared with PITX2 variants.

evidence.reference_title: Axenfeld-Rieger syndrome: report on dental and craniofacial findings.
evidence.snippet: Axenfeld-Rieger syndrome is a rare autosomal dominant disorder characterized by various ocular and extraocular malformations. Dental abnormalities are considered as definitive features for the diagnosis and differentiation of Rieger syndrome from other anterior chamber of the eye malformations.

evidence.reference_title: Enamel defects of Axenfeld-Rieger syndrome and the role of PITX2 in its pathogenesis.
evidence.snippet: Enamel formation is disturbed in some patients with ARS. Pitx2 knockdown can influence the proliferation and ameloblast differentiation of inner enamel epithelial cells, and PITX2 may regulate cell proliferation via Wnt/beta-catenin signaling pathway.

evidence.reference_title: Axenfeld-Rieger syndrome: new perspectives.
evidence.snippet: In the eye, this condition manifests with varying degrees of anterior segment dysgenesis and carries a high risk of glaucoma.

evidence.reference_title: Anterior segment dysgenesis and the developmental glaucomas are complex traits.
evidence.snippet: Individuals with malformations of structures of the anterior segment of the eye frequently develop elevated intraocular pressure and glaucoma.

evidence.reference_title: Early-onset glaucoma in Axenfeld-Rieger anomaly: long-term surgical results and visual outcome.
evidence.snippet: To determine the long-term surgical and visual outcomes in Indian children with early-onset glaucoma associated with Axenfeld-Rieger anomaly (ARA).

evidence.reference_title: FOXC1 variant in a family with anterior segment dysgenesis and normal-tension glaucoma.
evidence.snippet: Our study describes the glaucoma phenotype in a family with Axenfeld-Rieger syndrome (ARS) and a FOXC1 variant.

evidence.reference_title: Ocular hypertension in Axenfeld-Rieger Syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Glaucoma is the main cause of visual morbidity in patients with ARS, therefore a complete periodic ophthalmological exam is a priority.

evidence.reference_title: Unusual presentation in Axenfeld-Rieger syndrome.
evidence.snippet: The patient had megalocornea with Haab's striae in the right eye and posterior embryotoxon in both the eyes. In the left eye, there was a white cord-like structure traversing the anterior chamber with adhesions to iris tissue along its course.

evidence.reference_title: PITX2 gain-of-function in Rieger syndrome eye model.
evidence.snippet: The eye manifests with iris ruptures, irido-corneal adhesions, cloudy corneas, and glaucoma.

evidence.reference_title: The 6p25 deletion syndrome: An update on a rare neurocristopathy.
evidence.snippet: Key clinical features comprise facial dysmorphism including mid-face and dental hypoplasia, hearing loss, cardiac anomalies, and involuted periumbilical skin.

evidence.reference_title: Sensorineural hearing loss and hypoplastic cochlea in Axenfeld-Rieger syndrome with FOXC1 mutation.
evidence.snippet: Axenfeld-Rieger syndrome (ARS) type 3 is a rare autosomal dominant disease, characterized by anterior segment dysgenesis of the eye, hearing loss, and cardiac defects.

evidence.reference_title: Axenfeld-Rieger Syndrome.
evidence.snippet: This anomaly encompasses posterior embryotoxon, iris hypoplasia, polycoria, and corectopia.

evidence.reference_title: Variable Anterior Segment Dysgenesis and Cardiac Anomalies Caused by a Novel Truncating Variant of FOXC1.
evidence.snippet: Anterior segment dysgenesis (ASD) encompasses a wide spectrum of developmental abnormalities of the anterior ocular segment, including congenital cataract, iris hypoplasia, aniridia, iridocorneal synechiae, as well as Peters, Axenfeld, and Rieger anomalies.

evidence.reference_title: Screening for mutations of Axenfeld-Rieger syndrome caused by FOXC1 gene in Japanese patients.
evidence.snippet: In pedigrees 1,2, and 4, younger generations had iris hypoplasia with severe early-onset glaucoma, whereas their parents had posterior embryotoxon without glaucoma.

nodes.description: Improper development of the anterior segment of the eye, leading to issues such as iris defects and abnormal angle structures.

evidence.reference_title: Ophthalmological Manifestations of Axenfeld-Rieger Syndrome: Current Perspectives.
evidence.snippet: Axenfeld-Rieger syndrome (ARS) is a rare congenital disease that is primarily characterized by ocular anterior segment anomalies but is also associated with craniofacial, dental, cardiac, and neurologic abnormalities.

evidence.reference_title: Anterior segment dysgenesis and the developmental glaucomas are complex traits.
evidence.snippet: Individuals with malformations of structures of the anterior segment of the eye frequently develop elevated intraocular pressure and glaucoma.

evidence.reference_title: Variable Anterior Segment Dysgenesis and Cardiac Anomalies Caused by a Novel Truncating Variant of FOXC1.
evidence.snippet: Anterior segment dysgenesis (ASD) encompasses a wide spectrum of developmental abnormalities of the anterior ocular segment, including congenital cataract, iris hypoplasia, aniridia, iridocorneal synechiae, as well as Peters, Axenfeld, and Rieger anomalies.

evidence.reference_title: Aniridia and Axenfeld-Rieger Syndrome: Clinical presentations, molecular genetics and current/emerging therapies.
evidence.snippet: Aniridia and Axenfeld-Rieger Syndrome are related, human ocular disorders that are typically inherited in an autosomal dominant manner. Both result from incorrect development of the eye and have, as their most serious consequences, elevated risk to develop the blinding condition glaucoma.

evidence.reference_title: A Novel Mutation in PITX2 in a Patient with Axenfeld-Rieger Syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Axenfeld-Rieger syndrome is a rare autosomal dominant condition... A variant of uncertain significance in PITX2 was identified... it is suggested that this variant can be classified as pathogenic.

evidence.reference_title: Axenfeld-Rieger syndrome: A systematic review examining genetic, neurological, and neurovascular associations to inform screening.
evidence.snippet: Mutations in the transcription factors FOXC1 or PITX2 are the most well-studied genetic manifestations of this syndrome.

evidence.reference_title: Genotype-phenotype association of PITX2 and FOXC1 in Axenfeld-Rieger syndrome.
evidence.snippet: PITX2 and FOXC1 are the most common pathogenic genes associated with Axenfeld-Rieger syndrome (ARS).

evidence.reference_title: The molecular basis of Rieger syndrome. Analysis of Pitx2 homeodomain protein activities.
evidence.snippet: Mutations in the Pitx2 homeobox gene have been linked to Rieger syndrome.

evidence.reference_title: Posterior embryotoxon may not be a forme fruste of Axenfeld-Rieger's Syndrome.
evidence.snippet: One of the main ocular features associated with Axenfeld-Rieger is posterior embryotoxon, which is a prominent anteriorly displaced Schwalbe's line. This can be found in up to 15% of normal eyes, without any clinical significance or may represent a forme fruste of an anterior segment dysgenesis.

evidence.reference_title: Unusual presentation in Axenfeld-Rieger syndrome.
evidence.snippet: The patient had megalocornea with Haab's striae in the right eye and posterior embryotoxon in both the eyes.

evidence.reference_title: Hyperproliferative embryotoxon simulating double cornea.
evidence.snippet: Posterior embryotoxon, an anteriorly displaced Schwalbe's line, is the most common feature of Axenfeld Rieger syndrome.

evidence.reference_title: Posterior embryotoxon as the initial ophthalmological sign of Axenfeld-Rieger syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Posterior embryotoxon as the initial ophthalmological sign of Axenfeld-Rieger syndrome.

nodes.description: Involves multiple systems, often including dental, craniofacial, and umbilical abnormalities.

evidence.reference_title: Axenfeld-Rieger syndrome: report on dental and craniofacial findings.
evidence.snippet: Axenfeld-Rieger syndrome is a rare autosomal dominant disorder characterized by various ocular and extraocular malformations. Dental abnormalities are considered as definitive features for the diagnosis and differentiation of Rieger syndrome from other anterior chamber of the eye malformations.

evidence.reference_title: Cardiac anomalies in Axenfeld-Rieger syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Axenfeld-Rieger syndrome is a rare multi-system disorder associated with cardiac anomalies.

evidence.reference_title: The genetic basis of inherited anomalies of the teeth. Part 2: syndromes with significant dental involvement.
evidence.snippet: We will consider successively syndromic forms of amelogenesis imperfecta or enamel defects, dentinogenesis imperfecta (i.e. osteogenesis imperfecta) and other dentine anomalies.

evidence.reference_title: Analysis of mutations of the PITX2 transcription factor found in patients with Axenfeld-Rieger syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Two homeobox mutations, R43W and R90C, resulted in severely reduced DNA-binding and transcriptional activation despite normal nuclear localization. L105V, located C-terminal to the homeodomain, resulted in normal localization, reporter gene transactivation, and protein half-life, but with an altered mobility shift pattern of protein-DNA complexes. N108T, also located C-terminal to the homeodomain, resulted in an altered mobility shift pattern and with slightly increased reporter transactivation and shortened protein half-life.

evidence.reference_title: A Novel Mutation in PITX2 in a Patient with Axenfeld-Rieger Syndrome.
evidence.snippet: The mutation, NM_153427.2:c.272G>A (p.Arg91Gln), is predicted to be damaging by PolyPhen-2 (score of 0.997), identified as a missense mutation with an allele frequency of 1.648e-05 by the Exome Aggregation Consortium, and has been reported in ClinVar once, by the laboratory that analyzed our patient's sample.

evidence.reference_title: The molecular basis of Rieger syndrome. Analysis of Pitx2 homeodomain protein activities.
evidence.snippet: We next studied two Rieger mutants. A threonine to proline mutation (T68P) in the second helix of the homeodomain retained DNA binding activity with the same apparent KD and only about a 2-fold reduction in the Bmax. However, this mutant did not transactivate reporter genes containing the bicoid site.
